title: Data-Centric AI
description: Andrew Ng's data-centric approach: improving data quality, weak supervision, active learning, and data debugging often give more ROI than model improvements.
details: Data-Centric AI shifts focus from improving the model code to improving the data. When your model plateaus, better data often beats a better architecture. This file covers label quality and annotator agreement, data augmentation strategies (mixup, RandAugment), weak supervision with Snorkel (labeling functions, label model), active learning (uncertainty sampling, BALD), data debugging with confident learning (cleanlab), and the decision framework for choosing between data-centric and model-centric improvements. Every section includes FAANG interview questions that test practical data debugging judgment.

sections.title: Data Quality & Label Consistency
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Your model can't learn what the labels don't teach. If 10% of training labels are wrong, the best possible accuracy is 90% — no amount of architecture tuning fixes bad labels.

Label noise types:

  • Random noise: Symmetric (flip label with probability p) — surprisingly robust, model can learn with up to 40% random noise.
  • Systematic noise: Biased annotators consistently mislabel a class — much more damaging. Caused by ambiguous labeling guidelines, class imbalance, annotator fatigue.

Metrics: Cohen's κ (2 annotators), Fleiss' κ (3+). κ adjusts for chance agreement. κ < 0.4 means the label definition needs fixing.
sections.codeBlock:
from sklearn.metrics import cohen_kappa_score
from statsmodels.stats.inter_rater import fleiss_kappa
import numpy as np

# Cohen's kappa: 2 annotators
annotator1 = [0, 1, 1, 0, 1, 0, 1, 1]
annotator2 = [0, 1, 0, 0, 1, 0, 1, 0]
kappa = cohen_kappa_score(annotator1, annotator2)
print(f'Cohen\'s κ: {kappa:.3f}')

# Fleiss' kappa: n annotators, m categories
# ratings[i][j] = count of annotators that assigned category j to item i
ratings = np.array([
    [3, 1, 0],  # item 0: 3 annotators said class 0, 1 said class 1
    [2, 2, 0],
    [4, 0, 0],
    [1, 2, 1],
])
fkappa = fleiss_kappa(ratings)
print(f'Fleiss\' κ: {fkappa:.3f}')

sections.title: Data Augmentation
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Encode the invariances you want the model to learn. A cat rotated 15° is still a cat — so show the model rotated cats during training. This teaches translation/rotation invariance without needing more labeled data.

Common augmentations:

  • Image: rotation, flip, crop, color jitter, cutout (mask random square), mixup (linear interpolation of two images and their labels)
  • Text: back-translation, synonym replacement, random insertion/deletion (EDA — Easy Data Augmentation)
  • Audio: time stretch, pitch shift, noise addition, SpecAugment (mask frequency/time bands)

Advanced: AutoAugment learns the optimal augmentation policy via RL. RandAugment simplifies this by randomly picking N augmentations with fixed magnitude M — both as good as learned policies. Mixup with α=0.2 produces virtual training examples that act as a regularizer.
sections.codeBlock:
import tensorflow as tf
import numpy as np

# RandAugment-style: random augmentations
data_augmentation = tf.keras.Sequential([
    tf.keras.layers.RandomFlip('horizontal'),
    tf.keras.layers.RandomRotation(0.1),
    tf.keras.layers.RandomZoom(0.1),
    tf.keras.layers.RandomContrast(0.1),
])

# Mixup: interpolate between images and labels
def mixup(x, y, alpha=0.2):
    batch_size = tf.shape(x)[0]
    lam = tf.random.uniform((batch_size, 1), 0, 1)
    lam = tf.maximum(lam, 1 - lam)  # ensure lam >= 0.5
    lam_x = tf.reshape(lam, (-1, 1, 1, 1))
    indices = tf.random.shuffle(tf.range(batch_size))
    mixed_x = lam_x * x + (1 - lam_x) * tf.gather(x, indices)
    mixed_y = lam * y + (1 - lam) * tf.gather(y, indices)
    return mixed_x, mixed_y

# Using albumentations library for images
# import albumentations as A
# transform = A.Compose([
#     A.HorizontalFlip(p=0.5),
#     A.Rotate(limit=15, p=0.5),
#     A.CoarseDropout(max_holes=8, max_height=16, max_width=16),
# ])

sections.title: Weak Supervision (Snorkel)
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: You have 100K unlabeled examples but no budget for hand-labeling. Write 10 simple heuristic rules (labeling functions) — regex patterns, knowledge base lookups, heuristics — and Snorkel learns which rules are reliable and which aren't.

[Practical Workflow] Workflow:

  1. Write 10-30 labeling functions (LFs) that each take unlabeled data → {0, 1, -1 (abstain)}
  2. Snorkel's generative label model learns LF accuracies and correlations (no ground truth needed!)
  3. Output probabilistic training labels for each data point
  4. Train any downstream discriminative model on these weak labels

[Concrete Numbers] Concrete: Snorkel labeled 10K medical records with 15 regex LFs, achieving AUC 0.93 vs 0.87 for hand-labeling 1K records (Snorkel paper, Ratner et al. 2017).
sections.codeBlock:
# pip install snorkel
from snorkel.labeling import labeling_function, PandasLFApplier
from snorkel.labeling.model import LabelModel

# Define labeling functions (LFs) — heuristics that vote on labels
@labeling_function()
def lf_contains_urgent(x):
    # Heuristic: if 'urgent' appears, likely positive
    return 1 if 'urgent' in x.text.lower() else -1  # -1 = abstain

@labeling_function()
def lf_regex_phone(x):
    # Heuristic: phone number pattern -> likely spam
    import re
    return 1 if re.search(r'\(\d{3}\) \d{3}-\d{4}', x.text) else -1

@labeling_function()
def lf_keyword_offer(x):
    # Heuristic: discount keywords
    keywords = ['free', 'offer', 'limited time', 'discount']
    return 1 if any(k in x.text.lower() for k in keywords) else -1

# Apply LFs to unlabeled dataset
lfs = [lf_contains_urgent, lf_regex_phone, lf_keyword_offer]
applier = PandasLFApplier(lfs)
L_train = applier.apply(df_unlabeled)

# Train label model (learns LF accuracies)
label_model = LabelModel(cardinality=2, verbose=True)
label_model.fit(L_train, n_epochs=500, seed=123)

# Get probabilistic training labels
probs_train = label_model.predict_proba(L_train)

# Train downstream model on weak labels
y_weak = probs_train[:, 1] > 0.5
# model.fit(X, y_weak)

sections.title: Active Learning
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Instead of labeling random examples, let the model ask for labels on the examples it's most uncertain about. This reduces labeling effort by 50-80%.

Query strategies:

  • Uncertainty sampling: Query points where the model is least confident. Least confident: 1 - max(P(y|x)). Margin: P(y₁|x) - P(y₂|x). Entropy: -ΣP(y|x)·log(P(y|x)).
  • Diversity sampling: Query representative points that cover the feature space (coreset selection). Avoids redundant queries.
  • BALD (Bayesian Active Learning by Disagreement): Use MC dropout to estimate model uncertainty. Query points with highest disagreement across stochastic forward passes.

[Practical Workflow] Workflow: Label 1K examples → train initial model → score unlabeled pool → label top-100 uncertain → retrain → repeat until budget exhausted or performance plateaus.
sections.codeBlock:
# Simple active learning with modAL library
# pip install modAL

from modAL import ActiveLearner
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier

# Initial labeled data (small)
X_initial, y_initial = X_labeled[:100], y_labeled[:100]

# Active learner
learner = ActiveLearner(
    estimator=RandomForestClassifier(),
    query_strategy='uncertainty_sampling',
    X_training=X_initial, y_training=y_initial
)

# Active learning loop
n_queries = 10
for i in range(n_queries):
    query_idx, query_inst = learner.query(X_unlabeled, n_instances=10)
    # Get oracle label for queried instance
    y_new = oracle(query_inst)
    # Teach the model
    learner.teach(query_inst, y_new)
    # Remove queried instances from unlabeled pool
    X_unlabeled = np.delete(X_unlabeled, query_idx, axis=0)
    print(f'Round {i+1}: accuracy = {learner.score(X_test, y_test):.3f}')

# Manual uncertainty sampling
probas = model.predict_proba(X_unlabeled)
entropy = -np.sum(probas * np.log(probas + 1e-10), axis=1)
query_idx = np.argsort(entropy)[-10:]  # top-10 most uncertain

sections.title: Data Debugging
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: When a model makes a mistake, is it a model error (wrong prediction) or a data error (wrong label)? Confident learning systematically identifies mislabeled data.

[Practical Workflow] Workflow: Model at 85% test accuracy → examine misclassified training examples → if >5% have wrong labels, clean data first before improving the model.

Confident Learning (Northcutt et al., 2021):

  • Builds a count matrix C[observed, predicted] between observed labels and predicted labels (via cross-validation)
  • Computes per-class error bounds using the confident joint
  • Returns a ranked list of most likely label errors
  • Works with any classifier — it's a post-hoc analysis, not a training method

[Concrete Numbers] Concrete: On CIFAR-10, confident learning identified 2.5% label errors in the test set. Fixing those 250 labels improved accuracy by 1.5% — more than any architecture change gave that year.
sections.codeBlock:
# pip install cleanlab

from cleanlab import CleanLearning
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression

# Confident learning: find label errors
cl = CleanLearning(
    LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000),
    cv_n_folds=5,    # cross-validation for out-of-sample predictions
    verbose=True
)

# Find label issues
label_issues = cl.find_label_issues(X, y)

# Top-k most likely mislabeled examples
worst_idxs = label_issues['label_quality'].argsort()[:10]
print(f'Top 10 likely label errors:')
for idx in worst_idxs:
    print(f'  Index {idx}: true_label={y[idx]}, '
          f'predicted_label={label_issues["predicted_label"][idx]}, '
          f'quality={label_issues["label_quality"][idx]:.3f}')

# Train on cleaned labels
cl.fit(X, y)  # automatically removes/weights bad labels
print(f'Clean test accuracy: {cl.score(X_test, y_test):.3f}')

sections.title: Data-centric vs Model-centric
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition (Andrew Ng): When data quality is the bottleneck, improving data gives more ROI than improving the model. Most ML teams are model-centric (spend 80% on architecture, 20% on data); they should flip this.

Decision framework:

  • Underfitting (train error high): Model-centric — increase capacity, better architecture, more features, longer training
  • Overfitting with clean labels: Model-centric — more regularization, more data, simpler model
  • Overfitting with noisy labels: Data-centric first — fix label errors, check data quality. If >5% training labels are wrong, cleaning data is more efficient than regularizing
  • Long-tail performance gap: Data-centric — add more data for rare classes, active learning on tail examples

sections.title: FAANG Interview Q&A
sections.description:
[Interview Angle] Q1: "Your model has 90% accuracy but you discover 10% of training labels are wrong. What do you do?" — Amazon L5

First, quantify: use confident learning to identify which specific training examples are likely mislabeled and estimate the true label noise rate. If the 10% noise is random (symmetric), models can often tolerate it — the ceiling is roughly 90% because the best possible accuracy can't exceed 100% - noise_rate. But if the noise is systematic (e.g., all cats labeled as dogs by one annotator), it's much more damaging. I'd: (1) use cleanlab to find and correct the most confident errors, (2) re-examine annotator guidelines where agreement is low (Fleiss' κ < 0.6), (3) consider label smoothing or loss reweighting to reduce reliance on noisy labels, and (4) if budget allows, re-label the most uncertain examples. The ROI on cleaning data is almost always higher than tuning the architecture.

[Interview Angle] Q2: "How would you label 1M unlabeled examples with a budget of $1000?" — Google L4

I'd use a tiered approach. First, spend $100 on 1K high-quality labels via active learning (uncertainty sampling) — train an initial model. Then use weak supervision: write 10-15 labeling functions covering common patterns, train a Snorkel label model to get probabilistic labels for the remaining data. The label model captures LF accuracies without needing ground truth. Validate the weak labels against the 1K gold-standard set. If weak label accuracy > 85%, train the final model on all 1M weakly-labeled examples. If accuracy is lower, spend another $200 on targeted labels for the most uncertain examples. This yields high coverage at 1-2¢ per example instead of $1.

[Interview Angle] Q3: "What augmentations would you use for medical images?" — Netflix L5

Medical imaging requires label-preserving augmentations. Safe: random flips, small rotations (≤15°), elastic deformations (tissue moves non-rigidly), brightness/contrast adjustment (scanner variability). Unsafe: vertical flips (X-rays have anatomical orientation — flipping changes left/right), large rotations (organs have expected orientations), extreme color shifts (diagnostic color matters). For histopathology, color normalization (stain variation between labs) is critical — use torchstain or StainTools. For segmentation, all geometric transforms must apply identically to image and mask. The key: consult with clinicians about which transforms preserve the diagnostic features — a flip that reverses text annotations would be catastrophic.

[Interview Angle] Q4: "What's the difference between active learning and weak supervision?" — Apple E4

Active learning selects which examples to label from an unlabeled pool by querying an oracle (usually a human annotator). The model chooses uncertain or diverse examples. The bottleneck is labeling budget — you minimize the number of queries. Weak supervision programmatically generates labels without human-in-the-loop. You write labeling functions (heuristics, rules, KB lookups) and combine them via a label model. The bottleneck is writing good LFs. They're complementary: use active learning when you have labeling budget and can query an oracle; use weak supervision when you have domain knowledge to write rules but no budget. In practice, combine both: use weak supervision for broad coverage and active learning for the remaining uncertain examples.

[Interview Angle] Q5: "You're building a production model at 95% accuracy. Stakeholders want 97%. Do you improve the model or the data?" — Google L5

Start with a data-centric diagnosis. (1) Error analysis on the 5% mistakes: what fraction are data errors (wrong labels, missing features, ambiguous examples)? If > 30% of errors are data issues, fix data first — this is the highest ROI. (2) Check the learning curves: is the model underfitting or overfitting? If underfitting (high bias), add capacity or features (model-centric). If overfitting (high variance with large train/test gap), add more data (data-centric) or regularize (model-centric). (3) Check if the 97% target needs more data for the long tail of rare cases — add active learning for low-confidence predictions. (4) If data is clean and the model is at capacity, switch to a stronger model architecture (e.g., XGBoost → transformer). In practice, the 95→97% jump usually requires both: cleaner data for the last 1% and a better model for the remaining 1%.

sections.title: References
sections.description:
• Snorkel paper — Ratner et al. (2017): "Snorkel: Rapid Training Data Creation with Weak Supervision"
  • Confident Learning — Northcutt, Jiang, Chuang (2021): "Confident Learning: Estimating Uncertainty in Dataset Labels"
  • Data-Centric AI Course — Andrew Ng (deeplearning.ai): free online course
  • AutoAugment paper — Cubuk et al. (2019): learning augmentation policies
  • Mixup paper — Zhang et al. (2018): "mixup: Beyond Empirical Risk Minimization"
  • Active Learning survey — Settles (2009): comprehensive review
